notes.anchor: On the eighth day before the Ides of December
notes.note: A Roman date reckoned inclusively backwards from a fixed point of the month: the Ides of December fell on the 13th, so the eighth day before (counting both endpoints) is 6 December. Cicero's letters date by Kalends, Nones, and Ides plus an inclusive count of days.

notes.anchor: longer than the decree of the Senate prescribed
notes.note: A senatus consultum of 53/52 BC (the Pompeian law on provinces) limited a governor's tenure; Cicero stresses he left Cilicia after exactly one year not by personal lobbying but in conformity with that decree.

notes.anchor: that man portrayed in the sixth book
notes.note: The idealized statesman (rector rei publicae) sketched in book six of Cicero's own De Re Publica, which closes with the Somnium Scipionis; Cicero fears the canvass for a triumph compromises that ideal.

notes.anchor: a triumph by canvass, and the commonwealth in freedom
notes.note: A triumph required a senatorial vote and so political solicitation (ambitio); Cicero sets the self-interested pursuit of that honour against acting freely for the Republic in the looming crisis.

Imperium is the formal power of military command held by a governor; retaining it past his province meant Cicero could not enter the city proper while awaiting a triumph, but kept him a player with troops as civil war neared.

notes.anchor: the ten tribunes of the plebs should propose a measure to allow his candidacy
notes.note: The law of the ten tribunes (52 BC) let Caesar stand for a second consulship in absentia without laying down his command; Cicero blames the Pompeians for the grant that made Caesar unstoppable.

notes.anchor: what will be done, when it has been said, `Speak, Marcus Tullius!'
notes.note: In senatorial debate the presiding magistrate called on members by rank to give their sententia ("dic, M. Tulli"); Atticus asks how Cicero will vote when his turn comes, and Cicero answers that he will side with Pompey.

notes.anchor: everyone who is pressed by debt
notes.note: Aes alienum (debt) was an acute political force in the late Republic; Cicero numbers the indebted among Caesar's natural partisans, as men with everything to gain from upheaval and a cancellation of debts.

notes.anchor: When Cato dressed him up with the supplication
notes.note: A supplicatio was a public thanksgiving voted by the Senate for a victory, often a prelude to a triumph; Cato, who had blocked such honours for Caesar, here helped vote one for Bibulus, exposing his partisanship.

notes.anchor: when Caelius has seized the Flumentane Gate
notes.note: The Porta Flumentana stood on the Tiber bank below the Capitol; Cicero jokes that if a speculator like Caelius can buy up prime Roman property, he too may as well acquire holdings at Puteoli.

notes.anchor: did not reckon the Piraeus to be a town
notes.note: Cicero debates whether the Piraeus, the port of Athens, is a town (oppidum) taking the bare accusative or a mere locality (locus) taking a preposition; the grammatical pedantry is a friendly jab at Atticus the connoisseur of Latinity.
